citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
The applicant did not perform, or communicate to the attending veterinarian, complete daily observations of a female adult
armadillo to assess her health and well-being and the animal was found deceased.
On January 31, 2026, an adult 5-year old female armadillo was found deceased in her enclosure. The facility was not
aware that the animal was critically ill. The following observations were not identified by the applicant:
** The animal's body condition had significantly decreased. According to medical records, she was previously "extremely
overweight", with visible subcutaneous fat around the rear end according to the applicant. Upon necropsy, the animal was
"very thin with very little if any subcutaneous/organ fat". The applicant also stated that upon picking the armadillo up after
she was deceased, the applicant observed a noticeable difference in her weight.
** The necropsy record showed the stomach was "2/3 full of shavings" but did not appear obstructed by the bedding. The
applicant did not notice the animal was eating her bedding.
The following observations were made by the applicant (according to records and verbal reports) but were not
communicated to the veterinarian until the animal was found deceased:
** The animal's activity had been reduced for the past few months preceding her death and she had been sleeping more.
This was unusual for her.
** The animal did not eat the large meal offered to her 7 days prior to her death or the large meal offered 3 days prior. The
applicant reported that she ate some small snacks in between, however necropsy results showed no food in the intestines
or stomach (only bedding).
The lack of complete daily observations and lack of timely communication with the attending veterinarian prevented
opportunity for diagnosis or treatment of this condition before the animal had died.
Correct by: Ensuring there is daily observation of all animals to assess their health and well-being, and ensuring there is a
mechanism of direct and frequent communication to the attending veterinarian so that timely and accurate information on
problems of animal health, behavior, and well-being is conveyed.
This is a 1st Re-License Inspection for a Class (C) License. The inspection and exit briefing were conducted with the
applicant.
All items must be in compliance within 2 more inspections or by Tuesday July 7, 2026, or the applicant will forfeit the
license fee and must wait 6 months to reapply. Please contact your inspector to schedule your next Re-License
Inspection.
Regulated activity can continue under your existing USDA license until it expires.
n

narrative:
2.40(b)(3)  Critical
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
The applicant did not perform, or communicate to the attending veterinarian, complete daily observations of a female adult 
armadillo to assess her health and well-being and the animal was found deceased.  
 
On January 31, 2026, an adult 5-year old female armadillo was found deceased in her enclosure. The facility was not 
aware that the animal was critically ill. The following observations were not identified by the applicant:  
** The animal's body condition had significantly decreased. According to medical records, she was previously "extremely 
overweight", with visible subcutaneous fat around the rear end according to the applicant. Upon necropsy, the animal was 
"very thin with very little if any subcutaneous/organ fat". The applicant also stated that upon picking the armadillo up after 
she was deceased, the applicant observed a noticeable difference in her weight. 
** The necropsy record showed the stomach was "2/3 full of shavings" but did not appear obstructed by the bedding. The 
applicant did not notice the animal was eating her bedding. 
 
The following observations were made by the applicant (according to records and verbal reports) but were not 
communicated to the veterinarian until the animal was found deceased: 
** The animal's activity had been reduced for the past few months preceding her death and she had been sleeping more. 
This was unusual for her. 
** The animal did not eat the large meal offered to her 7 days prior to her death or the large meal offered 3 days prior. The 
applicant reported that she ate some small snacks in between, however necropsy results showed no food in the intestines 
or stomach (only bedding). 
 
The lack of complete daily observations and lack of timely communication with the attending veterinarian prevented 
opportunity for diagnosis or treatment of this condition before the animal had died.  
 
Correct by: Ensuring there is daily observation of all animals to assess their health and well-being, and ensuring there is a 
mechanism of direct and frequent communication to the attending veterinarian so that timely and accurate information on 
problems of animal health, behavior, and well-being is conveyed.

This is a 1st Re-License Inspection for a Class (C) License. The inspection and exit briefing were conducted with the 
applicant. 
 
All items must be in compliance within 2 more inspections or by Tuesday July 7, 2026, or the applicant will forfeit the 
license fee and must wait 6 months to reapply. Please contact your inspector to schedule your next Re-License 
Inspection. 
 
Regulated activity can continue under your existing USDA license until it expires.
